fix_plans.title: Switch to AdversarialTrainingPlan with batch classifier
fix_plans.content: Round 3 produced mean kBET 0.509 but per-batch acceptance 0.0/0.0 at every epoch including the final one — a smoking gun that the encoder is still perfectly separating batches in latent space (the GLOBAL null is met ~50% of the time only because the subsample itself is ~51/49 batch-mixed, not because neighbourhoods are mixed). Capacity tweaks (round 2: n_latent=30/n_hidden=256/dropout=0) regressed the metric to 0.271; simply extending to 50 epochs (round 3) climbed to 0.509 but plateaued below the 0.55 acceptable floor, and per-batch kBET stayed at 0.0/0.0 throughout. The metric is still rising at epoch 49 so more epochs alone might cross 0.55, but it will not reach the 0.80 standard threshold without directly attacking batch separation in the latent. The cleanest fix is to swap scVI's default TrainingPlan for scvi.train.AdversarialTrainingPlan with adversarial_classifier=True and scale_adversarial_loss='auto' (the adversarial loss scales from 1 down to 0 over the KL warmup, pushing the encoder to mix batches early when KL weight is low and reconstruction is the dominant signal). Concretely, edit train.py main() as follows: (1) at the top of main(), after building the SCVI model but before calling vae.train(), monkey-patch the class attribute: `scvi.model.SCVI._training_plan_cls = scvi.train.AdversarialTrainingPlan`. (This is the documented extension point — SCVI.train() does `training_plan = self._training_plan_cls(self.module, **plan_kwargs)` so overriding the class attribute is the supported way to swap plans without rewriting the train() call.) (2) Update the existing plan_kwargs=... argument in vae.train(...) to: `plan_kwargs={'lr': args.lr, 'n_epochs_kl_warmup': 5, 'adversarial_classifier': True, 'scale_adversarial_loss': 'auto'}`. Do NOT change max_epochs, batch_size, n_latent, n_hidden, dropout_rate, the record()/conclude() call lines, the _ProbeCallback, or prober.py. Do NOT add a new argparse arg. Rationale: AdversarialTrainingPlan adds a small Classifier head on the latent that tries to predict batch identity from z, and the VAE's loss is augmented with `(1 - kl_weight) * fool_loss` — the encoder is penalized whenever the classifier succeeds, so it learns to make z batch-invariant. This is the canonical scib-metrics trick for pushing per-batch kBET off 0.0/0.0 when standard scVI stalls. Expected outcome: per-batch kBET rises from 0.0/0.0 to >0.3 within ~20 epochs as the adversarial signal forces mixing; mean kBET should cross 0.55 (acceptable) by epoch ~30 and approach 0.7-0.85 by epoch 50. ELBO anchor: the adversarial loss slightly slows reconstruction convergence, so elbo_train and elbo_validation will be a few percent higher than round 3's 4785/4883, but both will still decrease monotonically across epochs, satisfying the utility floor (the anchor guard reverts only on >20% degradation).

fix_plans.title: Force fully batch-balanced 12000-cell subsample
fix_plans.content: Round 3 plateaued at tail_mean 0.495 with per-batch kBET 0.0/0.0 — the GLOBAL null is met ~50% of the time but every per-batch restricted test fails. With --max-cells=8000 and --batch-balance-strength=0.5, sample_cells() draws a subsample whose batch proportions are (1-0.5)*[0.523, 0.477] + 0.5*[0.5, 0.5] = [0.5115, 0.4885] — close to balanced but not 50/50. More importantly, with only ~4094 cells from batch 0 and ~3906 from batch 1, k=50 neighbourhoods are small enough that ANY residual batch structure in the latent produces fully-separated neighbourhoods, which is exactly what per-batch kBET=0.0/0.0 indicates. This data-handling angle has NOT been tried: round 2 widened the model (regressed), round 3 extended training (improved but plateaued). Forcing a 50/50 subsample of 12000 cells directly attacks the per-batch kBET=0.0 root cause: when the global composition is exactly 50/50 AND each batch contributes ~6000 cells, k=50 neighbourhoods have enough statistical mass that random mixing is achievable, and kBET's null hypothesis becomes reachable. Concretely, edit train.py build_parser() defaults: change `--max-cells` from 8000 to 12000, and change `--batch-balance-strength` from 0.5 to 1.0. With balance_strength=1.0, sample_cells() sets target_proportions = uniform = [0.5, 0.5], so each batch contributes exactly 6000 cells (capped by min(6000, 7378) and min(6000, 8098) respectively — both fit, so the subsample is genuinely 6000/6000). Do NOT touch n_latent, n_hidden, dropout_rate, train_epochs, lr, the vae.train() kwargs, the record()/conclude() call lines, _ProbeCallback, or prober.py. Rationale: round 3's per-batch kBET=0.0/0.0 with rising mean kBET (0.319 -> 0.509) shows the encoder is mixing some cells but the per-batch neighbourhood test still fails every time because the GLOBAL composition is 51/49 not 50/50 — kBET's null expects each neighbourhood to match the global proportion, so a 51/49 global makes the test asymmetric and easy to fail for the smaller batch. Forcing 50/50 fixes the global composition so kBET's null is symmetric and reachable. Expected outcome: per-batch kBET should rise from 0.0/0.0 to >0.3 as the symmetric global composition lets the latent's residual mixing show through; mean kBET should cross 0.55 (acceptable) within 30 epochs and approach 0.7+ by epoch 50. ELBO anchor unaffected — training loss is computed per-batch on the full minibatch, not on global composition.

fix_plans.title: Enable encode_covariates + deep injection + per-batch dispersion
fix_plans.content: Round 3 still has per-batch kBET 0.0/0.0 at epoch 49, meaning the encoder produces a latent that perfectly separates batches. The root cause is architectural: by default scVI's VAE has encode_covariates=False, so the batch one-hot is fed ONLY to the decoder (via the batch-specific decoder bias), never to the encoder. The encoder must therefore infer batch identity from gene expression alone, which forces it to embed batch-correlated signals into the latent — the opposite of what we want for batch mixing. Round 2's widening (n_latent=30, n_hidden=256, dropout=0) regressed the metric to 0.271 because it gave the encoder MORE capacity to encode batch-specific signals without fixing this fundamental information-flow bug. Round 3's 50-epoch extension climbed to 0.509 because the KL warmup eventually pulls the latent toward the prior, but it cannot fix the architectural mismatch. This model-architecture angle has NOT been tried in any prior round. Concretely, edit train.py main() SCVI construction to: `vae = scvi.model.SCVI(adata, n_latent=args.n_latent, n_hidden=args.n_hidden, n_layers=2, dropout_rate=args.dropout_rate, encode_covariates=True, deeply_inject_covariates=True, use_layer_norm='both', dispersion='gene-batch')`. These are all VAE.__init__ kwargs that flow through SCVI's **kwargs. Do NOT add new argparse args (these are constants inline). Do NOT change max_epochs, lr, plan_kwargs, batch_size, max_cells, batch_balance_strength, the record()/conclude() call lines, _ProbeCallback, or prober.py. Rationale: encode_covariates=True feeds the batch one-hot to the encoder's FCLayers; deeply_inject_covariates=True injects it into every hidden layer (not just the input), so the encoder can subtract batch effects before producing z. dispersion='gene-batch' lets each gene have a separate dispersion parameter per batch, removing a batch-driven residual that the encoder would otherwise have to embed in z. use_layer_norm='both' stabilizes the deeper encoder (n_layers=2) so the deeper injection does not destabilize training. n_layers=2 gives the batch-correction head a richer representation to align batches without the round-2 failure mode (where wider single-layer + dropout=0 collapsed). Expected outcome: per-batch kBET rises from 0.0/0.0 to >0.4 within ~20 epochs as the encoder can now condition on batch directly; mean kBET should cross 0.55 (acceptable) by epoch ~25 and approach 0.75-0.85 by epoch 50. ELBO anchor: elbo_train and elbo_validation will be slightly higher in absolute terms (encode_covariates adds input dimensions to the encoder, slightly changing the ELBO scale), but both will still decrease monotonically across epochs, satisfying the utility floor.
